third, unconfirmed example in a European museum is rumored to exist.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), ND (ca. 1496-1497). Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), ND (ca. 1496-1497). Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans. 
NGC EF-45. Fr-4; Galster-27A; Sieg-10; Schou 3; Bruun-3840. Weight: 3.17 gms. Mintmaster: Herman 
van Nassau or Hans Sever. A wonderful example of one of Denmark’s first gold coins, struck to finance the 
German mercenaries in the war against the Swedish rebel king, Sten Gustafsson Sture. Displaying a handsome 
retained brilliance, with only light instances of actual circulation.  
The coin was formerly in the collection of the legal councillor Frederik Christian Bech (1817-1905) who acted as 
a judge in

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler (Prinsens Daler), 1590. Christian IV . NGC EF-40.  Dav-8230; Hede-43; Sieg-
93; Schou-1; Bruun-4594. Weight: 29.04 gms. A choice, nicely toned and highly detailed example of the  
RARE so-called “prinsedaler” (Prince Taler), which was struck on application from Gyrvhild Fadersdatter 
and Peder Brahe. The 3/4 profile portrait of the only 13 year old king wearing a ruff is very unusual in Danish 
numismatics but seems to be copied after a portrait by Hans Knieper, still in the Royal collection at Rosenborg 
Castle in Copenhagen. The surfaces are yet attractive, with a steely

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser (2-1/2 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV .
denmark.body_excerpt:

Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. Only two examples known to exist, the present and the one in the National 
Museum of Denmark. Although the flan is slightly wavy, the overall condition is appealing with exquisite 
details and traces of original luster. 
This interesting and otherwise completely unobtainable issue was struck on the orders of the dowager queen 
Sophia on behalf of her son, who was only 16 at

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Ungersk Gylden), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV . NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:

Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. EXTREMELY RARE and quite presentable, with the noted cleaning being 
limited. Struck for the dowager queen, Sophia. 
The engraver copied the design of the Hungarian model too closely when he showed the elected king Christian

denmark.body_excerpt:

nicely toned and highly lustrous example, boldly struck with an insignificant die-break at the lower reverse.
This beautiful broad Krone (Corona Danica) is in many ways the archetypical coin of Christian IV . The 
denomination, inspired by the English Crown, was introduced in 1618 with the intention that it should pass 
for 1-½ Speciedaler but the king had arranged for the silver to be debased

examples this lovely piece is UNIQUE in private hands. The luster is bright and brilliant, with a generally 
sharp strike that only shows limited weakness. Entirely appealing and deserving of much attention from the 
numerous specialists seeking out an example of this type. 
When the new silver Krone was introduced in 1618 it was at the same time decided that an issue with the same 
value (1-1/2

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, ND (1607-11). Elsinore Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62.  KM-147; Dav-
denmark.body_excerpt:

LS529; Hede-63D; Sieg-113.2; Schou-20; Bruun-5846. Weight: 56.36 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Flemming. A

period it was run by the Dutchman, Hans Flemming.

DENMARK. Ducat, 1694. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-66 Prooflike. Fr-161; KM-433; Hede-
41; Sieg-68; Schou-6; Kold-138c; Bruun-7163. Weight: 3.50 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: 
Anton Meybusch. Brightly lustered and sharply distinctive in its cameo appeal, this Ducat glows with an 
almost entrancing eye appeal. The details are well formed and expressive, and the watery fields are rich with 
sharp reflectivity. Signs of marks or handling are very limited, and scarcely worth a mention. A Prooflike 
example that presents incredibly nicely, and one that is doubtlessly worth a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 3 Krone, 1695. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62.  KM-430; Dav-3646; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 3 Krone, 1695. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62.  KM-430; Dav-3646; Hede-
101; Sieg-53; Schou-14; Kold-126d; Bruun-7191. Weight: 54.37 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke (CW). 
Engraver: Anton Meybusch. EXTREMELY RARE, with a search yielding no other examples of this date 
trading or being held in private collections. Given this, one might be tempted to take this coin no matter 
the condition, though fortunately that choice is not presented here, with this example being exemplary on 
technical preservation. Soft color has uncoiled itself across the surfaces, and handling is ver

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1696. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-55.  Fr-181; KM-446; Hede-47;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1696. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-55.  Fr-181; KM-446; Hede-47; 
Sieg-79; Schou-4; Bruun-7205. Weight: 7.00 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. One of only two recorded 
examples, the other one being in the National Museum of Denmark. Although minor marks can be seen in 
the otherwise shimmering fields, the strike and the overall condition is excellent. A truly impressive example 
that presents very well as a nearly-mint piece. 
The dies were engraved for a 1 Ducat (Hede 46) which is also very rare. In the far background below the horse a 
city-view can just be seen

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1699. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64. Fr-170; KM-453; Hede-53; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1699. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64. Fr-170; KM-453; Hede-53; Sieg-
111; Schou-3; Kold-147c; Bruun-7233. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton 
Meybusch. Condition-wise a marvelous near-Gem of borderline perfection, and on top of that a VERY 
RARE and historically significant piece.
On the stern of the ship lying in front of the Christiansborg fort in Guinea can be seen the initials, DWC (Dansk 
Westindisk Compagnie / Danish West India Company) of the trading company that had commissioned this issue.
The West India Company was formally chart

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Medallic 2 Ducats, ND (1670-99). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62.  Fr-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Medallic 2 Ducats, ND (1670-99). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62.  Fr-
Unlisted; KM-460; Hede-59; Sieg-126; Schou-7; JS-533; Bruun-7240. Weight: 6.91 gms. EXTREMELY RARE 
and with bright golden surfaces and a design that is captivating in its distinctiveness. 
The weight and overall fabric of the piece is consistent with a Double Ducat, but instead of mentioning the 
sovereign in the legend as usual, it reads: FVMO FLAMMA PROXIMA / SED SANGVINE CONSTANT which 
can be translated something like: “Fire follows smoke but the price will be bloody” (partly taken from the “Curc

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold 2 Ducats, ND (1670-99). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64.  Fr-204; KM-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold 2 Ducats, ND (1670-99). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64.  Fr-204; KM-
458; Hede-56; Sieg-114; Schou-12; Bruun-7243. Weight: 6.87 gms. EXCESSIVELY RARE with only a very 
few in private hands. A genuinely superb jewel of a coin on which the level of detail in the city view makes 
you dream you could be there.
Underneath the text “Hafnia Dania” (Copenhagen - Denmark) is shown a magnificent view of the capital seen 
from the harbor, from which it clearly emerges that it was not without a reason that Copenhagen was dubbed, 
“City of Towers”. The amusing obverse with the perso

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, ND (1685). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Fr-186; KM-374.2; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, ND (1685). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Fr-186; KM-374.2; Hede-
12B; Sieg-76; Schou-19; Bruun-7249. Weight: 3.43 gms. Mintmaster: Gregorius Sesemann. Fields gleaming 
with luster and almost frosted devices make this already RARE coin truly exceptional. Details are beautiful 
and impressively applied, with limited instances of actual handling or circulation. 
The initials D.G. in the center of the reverse is an unusually prominent placing of the fact that the absolute 
monarch was king by the grace of God (Dei Gratia).
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-209; KM-461; Hede-3;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-209; KM-461; Hede-3; 
Sieg-36; Schou-4; Kold-148c; Bruun-7291. Weight: 6.90 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton 
Meybusch.  A very pleasing example that accentuates the brilliant portraits of Meybusch.  EXTREMELY 
RARE with probably no more than four examples in private ownership. Struck in commemoration of the 
royal succession on August 25th 1699.
Christian V , who was an enthusiastic huntsman, died from injuries that were inflicted upon him by a stag, the 
antlers of which are still preserved in the Ro

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1701. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-229; KM-475; Hede-8; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1701. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-229; KM-475; Hede-8; Sieg-
49; Schou-1; Kold-153; Bruun-7305. Weight: 6.87 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton 
Meybusch. The double-monogram on the reverse is surrounded by the king’s royal motto: DOMINUS MIHI 
ADIUTOR (“The Lord be my helper”). An EXTREMELY RARE type and date with only 3-4 examples resting 
in private collections, the chances to acquire any example are necessarily elusive, making this offering one of 
critical importance to the collector of Danish coins. Brightly lustrous and alluring, this

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1701. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-60.  Fr-243; cf. KM-476; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1701. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-60.  Fr-243; cf. KM-476; Hede-
10; Sieg-50; Schou-2; Kold-154; Bruun-7306. Weight: 6.94 gms.  Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: 
Anton Meybusch. EXTREMELY RARE, with only a few examples trading at auction in the prior decades, 
this 2 Ducats stands as one of the most popular and desirable of issues from Scandinavia, given the links to 
the Danish colonial empire and the phenomenal reverse design. The surfaces are unimpeachably beautiful, 
displaying all the Mint State appeal that is to be expected from such a grade. The strik

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 5 Ducats, 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-63 Prooflike.  Fr-246; KM-482;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 5 Ducats, 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-63 Prooflike.  Fr-246; KM-482; 
Hede-12; Sieg-53; Schou-1; Bruun-7327. Weight: 17.12 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Mintage: 251. 
Coupling an EXTREME RARITY with a beautiful nautical theme on the reverse, this 5 Ducats reaches 
out and grabs one’s attention in a manner that is hard to describe and touches the aesthetic judgement in 
such a way that only the finest works of art can. The details are so finely engraved they can only be called 
a masterwork, being some of the finest style work seen on any contemporary issue. The s

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC Unc Details—Reverse Cleaned.  Fr-244;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC Unc Details—Reverse Cleaned.  Fr-244; 
KM-481; Hede-11A; Sieg-42.1; Schou-4; Bruun-7330. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. A 
VERY RARE Ducat featuring the Fort Christiansborg on the Gold Coast in West Africa. This example is 
nicely detailed, with no signs of actual handling and the noted cleaning designation arising from some stray 
lines on the reverse. Even though imperfect, this piece is eminently presentable.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-64.  KM-479.1; Dav-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-64.  KM-479.1; Dav-
1289; NMD-1C; Hede-38; Sieg-23.1; Schou-10; Bruun-7336. Weight: 26.79 gms. Mintmaster: Christian 
Wineke. Struck for the king’s travel to Norway which lasted from the 29th of May until the 5th of September 
1704. This Royal “Travel-Taler” bursts with engrossing eye appeal, delivering rich glossiness throughout the 
flan. Alluring tone is seen interspersed throughout, providing blue, ruby, and auburn highlights. An example 
that has both beauty and history, a formidable combination. Two minor planchet

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Danish West India Company. 2 Ducats, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Danish West India Company. 2 Ducats, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-
63. Fr-247; KM-A488; Hede-13; Sieg-51; Schou-1; Bruun-7352. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: Christian 
Wineke. A VERY RARE and beautiful type that was struck for the Danish West Indies Company and bearing 
a reverse legend can easily be seen as a glorification of the company itself: METIENDO MARIA AUGEMUR 
(“By sailing the Seas we enrich ourselves”). This interesting historical piece is supplemented by a heaping 
helping of beauty, with the surfaces glowing with a fiery and lustrous sheen. The details are

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1709. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-212; KM-485; Hede-14; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1709. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-212; KM-485; Hede-14; Sieg-
43; Schou-4; Bruun-7360. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. EXTREMELY RARE with only 
a very few in private collections, this Ducat featuring an alternating pattern of crowned arms and monograms 
delivers a charming eye appeal with rich luster and well articulated details. The nearly Choice surfaces radiate, 
with the overall appearance of the coin being close to a prooflike example. Evidence of marking or handling 
is limited, leaving a wholesome and largely intact surface quality.
€13,

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1710. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58.  Fr-237; KM-498; Hede-19;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1710. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58.  Fr-237; KM-498; Hede-19; 
Sieg-48; Schou-1; Bruun-7361. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Apart from this example, 
only the one in the National Museum of Denmark has so far been registered, highlighting the EXTREME 
RARITY, and consequently, demand for the date. The present Ducat is pleasingly detailed, with only light 
touches of circulation and nearly complete details. A reserved canary yellow color is seen on the surfaces, 
giving this piece a distinguished appeal and appearance.
€20,000-€25,000
From the L. E

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold 16 Skilling (2 Ducats), 1713. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Heart. Frederik IV . NGC MS-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold 16 Skilling (2 Ducats), 1713. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Heart. Frederik IV . NGC MS-
64. Fr-Unlisted; KM-Unlisted; Hede-47; Sieg-15; Schou-1a; Bruun-7396. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: 
Christian Wineke (CW). An intriguing and VERY RARE type, featuring a gold planchet struck to a Double 
Ducat weight standard with the dies customarily used for a 16 Skilling. The luster on this example is bright, 
with a stunning glossy appeal that dances in the light. The details are crisply laid, with no evidence of weakness 
or defect in strike. Only one further example in gold has been traced, as l

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Courant Ducat (Rixdaler), 1715. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Heart. Frederik IV . NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Courant Ducat (Rixdaler), 1715. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Heart. Frederik IV . NGC 
MS-65. Fr-222; KM-510; Hede-33; Sieg-25; Schou-2; Bruun-7423. Weight: 1.44 gms. Mintmaster: Christian 
Wineke (CW). Mintage: 598. A seldom encountered type, especially when considering the elevated grade 
status of the piece, with this piece being the sole specimen to reach the MS-65 threshold. The surfaces glow 
with an engaging effervescence of gold, marks are exceedingly difficult to pick out, even under magnification.
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Julius Gustav Berger Klemp Coll

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Krone, 1723. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-60.  KM-523; Dav-1290; Hede-39;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Krone, 1723. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-60.  KM-523; Dav-1290; Hede-39; 
Sieg-19; Schou-2; Bruun-7518. Weight: 22.36 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke (CW). Engraver: Olav Wif. 
Purported Mintage: 60. An EXTREMELY RARE date in enviable condition, this stunningly beautiful Krone 
delivers a magnificent appeal, with a bold strike and soft gleaming luster. Touches of handsome toning are 
seen in some protected design areas, with punctuating color supporting the general appeal of the example.
Although struck in Copenhagen, the dies were engraved by Olav Wif, who worked at Kongs

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 3 Krone, 1726. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-55. KM-525; Dav-1293; Hede-41;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 3 Krone, 1726. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-55. KM-525; Dav-1293; Hede-41; 
Sieg-22; Schou-2; Bruun-7524. Weight: 67.37 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Peter Berg. 
Conditionally elusive, this attractive 3 Krone delivers a splendid appeal, with only light touches of wear and 
soft gleaming allure. The strike is essentially faultless, with the intricate design being executed at a high level. 
The oblique milling on the edge confirms this piece’s status as an original striking, as later restrikes from the 
original dies can be distinguished on account of having a s

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, ND (1699-1730). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-64★. Fr-241; KM-452;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, ND (1699-1730). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-64★. Fr-241; KM-452; 
Hede-25; Sieg-29; Schou-13; Bruun-7557. Weight: 1.81 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. EXTREMELY 
RARE with only a very few in private hands, this fractional Ducat generates a tremendous eye appeal, with 
bright luster and an essentially prooflike finish. The well laid details are deeply frosted, with bold contrast 
between open field and design. Exceedingly alluring, as evidenced by the “star” modifier, highlighting 
exceptional eye appeal.
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.



Page 1

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1732. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-63+. Fr-249; KM-540; Hede-2; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1732. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-63+. Fr-249; KM-540; Hede-2; Sieg-
8; Schou-1; Bruun-7568. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Georg Wilhelm Wahl. 
Sharply struck and most desirous, this alluring Choice Ducat does not lack for eye appeal. Soft glossy luster 
wafts from the flan when held in the light, and evidence of contact marks or wispy handling is exceedingly 
limited. Featuring a reverse legend of “DEO ET POPVLO” (“God and the people”), the king’s royal motto, this 
Ducat has an added appeal. A Ducat that is short on faults, and not lackin

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Kronerigsdaler (6 Mark), 1732. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-64+. KM-539;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Kronerigsdaler (6 Mark), 1732. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-64+. KM-539; 
Dav-1295; NMD-1; Hede-5; Sieg-6; Schou-2; Bruun-7569. Weight: 26.84 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. 
Engraver: Georg Wilhelm Wahl. This enticing nearly-Gem “Travel-Taler” is detailed with a razor-sharp 
clarity that is cutting with precision. Light brilliance glows off the flan, with touches of onyx color mottled 
throughout. As one of the rarest dated “travel-talers” interest should be strong, especially when considering 
the stunning condition. 
The EXTREME RARITY of this Travel-Taler (Rejsedaler)

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1738. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-63.  Fr-248; KM-542; Hede-3;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1738. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VI. NGC MS-63.  Fr-248; KM-542; Hede-3; 
Sieg-9; Schou-1; Bruun-7585. Weight: 3.47 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. A transfixing Ducat, this 
representative featuring an intriguing reverse motif of Fort Christiansborg, with the crowned monogram of 
Christian VI on the obverse; a change of pace as most Danish gold contemporary with this example feature a 
portrait of the ruler. Beautiful, vibrant luster provides a reflective luster reminiscent of a mirror. Altogether, a 
representative deserving of an exultant position in the next cabinet it e

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, ND (1746). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65+ ★. Fr-251; KM-553;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, ND (1746). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65+ ★. Fr-251; KM-553; 
Hede-2A; Sieg-23.1; Schou-1; Bruun-7601. Weight: 6.96 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: 
Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). EXTREMELY RARE with only a very few in private hands, this stunning 
Double Ducat honoring the accession of Frederik V features a bust of Frederik V counterposed against a bust 
of the recently deceased Christian VI. This incredible dual portrait Ducat delivers entrancing brilliance, with 
deep reflective luster pooling in the open fields and hard frosted devices. Finding even a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65★. Fr-253; KM-555; Hede-4A;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65★. Fr-253; KM-555; Hede-4A; 
Sieg-25.1; Schou-4; Bruun-7604. Weight: 6.97 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus 
Arbien (A). Of the HIGHEST RARITY, this most pleasing Double Ducat features an appeal that combines 
occident and orient. The beautiful flan struck on Chinese gold displays impressive details, with bright details 
that are sparkling with frosty luster. The fields shine, and finding even a single fault is difficult. About as 
alluring as one could ever expect an example of this desirable type, and as s

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65.  Fr-257; KM-556; Hede-6;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65.  Fr-257; KM-556; Hede-6; 
Sieg-27; Schou-5; Bruun-7605. Weight: 6.91 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus 
Arbien (A). It goes without saying that this Double Ducat produced from gold sourced by the Danish Asiatic 
Trading Company from Qing China is EXTREMELY RARE. The reverse features the banner of the Asiatic 
Company set upon a Roman style galley, presenting an artful and stylized rendition of work of the Danish 
Asiatic Company. This Gem glitters with a notable brilliance, with enticing frosting on the de

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Fr-263; KM-558; Hede-8; Sieg-29;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Fr-263; KM-558; Hede-8; Sieg-29; 
Schou-6; Bruun-7606. Weight: 6.96 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). 
One of only two traced examples, with the other one being in the National Museum of Denmark. The elegantly 
composed reverse very unusually bears a legend in Danish instead of Latin, as “FRA CHRISTIANSBORG” 
means “From Christiansborg” i. e. referring to a scene from the harbor facing the fortress of Christiansborg on 
the cost of Guinea and reflected in the intricately detailed depiction of a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1747. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. KM-562; Dav-1299; Hede-25;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1747. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. KM-562; Dav-1299; Hede-25; 
Sieg-16; Schou-12; Bruun-7623. Weight: 29.03 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus 
Arbien (A). This especially attractive Speciedaler commemorating the anointment of Frederik V displays an 
intricate obverse featuring the new king enthroned. Brilliant glossiness shimmers and sheens from the flan, 
and any traces of marking are limited and unobtrusive. An example befitting the most regal of collections. 
Disregarding the essentially German Zweidrittel struck for Christian V in Ol

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1749. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-66+.  KM-575; Dav-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1749. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-66+.  KM-575; Dav-
1301B; NMD-1B; Hede-33B; Sieg-15.2; Schou-4; Bruun-7641. Weight: 27.17 gms. Mintmaster: Ingeborg 
Maria Wineke. Engraver: Johann Heinrich Wahl (W). This bright premium Gem delivers a shiny effervescence 
of bright luster, with a brilliant strike and limited flecks of mottled color and bright toning throughout. Struck 
as a “Travel Taler” (rejsedaler) for the king and his entourage for his journey to and stay in Norway in the 
summer of 1749, this piece has all the appeal and allure of freshly minted coi

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1756. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65 Deep Prooflike. Fr-268; KM-583;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1756. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65 Deep Prooflike. Fr-268; KM-583; 
Hede-19; Sieg-40; Schou-1; Bruun-7657. Weight: 3.50 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai van Hauen (VH). 
Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien. Perhaps the single most impressive survivor extant from this three year 
type, this magnificent Ducat displays stunning reflective qualities in the fields, while the designs are laden 
with the utmost frost. An absolute treasure to behold, the strike is also entirely complete, with the popular 
ship motif on the reverse seemingly floating atop the surfaces.
Bearing a s

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 12 Mark (Courant Ducat), 1765. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-66 Prooflike. Fr-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 12 Mark (Courant Ducat), 1765. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-66 Prooflike. Fr-
269; KM-587.7; Hede-22G; Sieg-21.7; Schou-1; Bruun-7728. Weight: 3.10 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven 
Knoph (HSK). Engraver: Daniel Jensen Adzer (DIA), who engraved a large number of medals but is known 
to have engraved only two coins: this and the Speciedaler from 1764 (Hede 27B). Quite the RARE Ducat, 
this tremendous example displays bright luster and a mirrored brilliance that is rather commendable. A 
slight prooflike appearance besets the surfaces, with gentle frosting on the devices in addit

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1769. Rethwisch Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-65. KM-607; Dav-1305; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1769. Rethwisch Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-65. KM-607; Dav-1305; Hede-
7; Sieg-35; Schou-1; FP-E6; Bruun-7748. Weight: 28.87 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven Knoph (HSK). 
Engraver: Johan Ephraim Bauert (B). Mintage: 51. Bauert’s portrait of King Christian VII is one of the most 
stylistically beautiful works of engraving, with the full richness of detail in the armored and gartered bust 
evident. This example does that engraving much justice, being struck on a highly engaging and lustrous 
flan that has escaped the ravages of time, being pleasingly free of handling and m

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1771. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-64+.  KM-623; Dav-1307;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1771. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-64+.  KM-623; Dav-1307; 
Hede-11A; Sieg-23.1; Schou-4; FP-36; Bruun-7757. Weight: 29.09 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven Knoph 
(HSK). Engraver: Johan Ephraim Bauert. Topping the NGC population report, this Speciedaler is richly 
lustrous and appealing, with sharp strike quality and alluring brilliance throughout. Touches of handsome 
tone support the argent brilliance, with a nice mottled contrast. A stunning example that will doubtlessly 
capture the attention of many collectors of conditional rarities.
€4,000-€6,000
From the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1799. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-65+.  KM-654; Dav-1315;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1799. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-65+.  KM-654; Dav-1315;

sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and 
gold coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt. The 2 Ducat

Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade 
in the center of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.

Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. KM-586; Fr-278; Hede-24B; 
Sieg-20.2; Schou-3; Bruun-7696. Weight: 3,05 gms. Mintmaster: 
Hans Schieven Knoph. Uncommon date. Delicate, untouched 
surfaces on this wonderful type with the three double mono -
grams and the radiant lines, hence the Danish moniker ‘Stråledu-
kat - radiance Ducat’ . 
€1,500-€1,800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Wonderful Near Gem Portrait Taler

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1764-B. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Dav-1302A; KM-599; Hede-27A;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1764-B. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Dav-1302A; KM-599; Hede-27A; 
Sieg-18.1; Schou-1; Bruun-7710. Weight: 29.07 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven Knoph. Engraver: Johan Ephraim 
Bauert. Incredibly lustrous and pristine near-Gem with a beautiful subtle toning.
The very characteristic portrait which bears reminiscence to J. J. Saly’s equestrian statue at Amalienborg Castle was created 
by the Swedish born engraver, J. E. Bauert who also provided dies for the production at Norway and Oldenburg.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/4 Speciedaler, 1769-HSK. Rethwisch Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-67. KM-604; Hede-10; Sieg-31;

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 12 Mark (Courant Ducat), 1771-K. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-66. Fr-280; KM-624;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 12 Mark (Courant Ducat), 1771-K. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-66. Fr-280; KM-624; 
Hede-3; Sieg-26; Schou-2; FP-E7; Bruun-7756. Weight: 3.11 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schivern Knoph. Engraver: Johan 
Ephraim Bauert. A superb Gem Uncirculated example of spectacular beauty. 
This beautiful one-year type was struck in only 131 pieces for the king’s birthday on the 29th of January. Most were used as 
largesse money at the riding court at Christiansborg Castle. 
The issue seems to have been instigated by the minister Johann Friedrich Struensee, who was also the mentally ill king’s ph

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1786. Altona / Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Dav-1310; KM-640.1;
denmark.body_excerpt:

Hede-22B; Sieg-18.2; Schou-1; FP-34.2; Lange- ; Bruun-7863. Weight: 28.02 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: 
Johan Henrik Wolff. Excellent condition for type.
These Speciedalers take their imagery from the Dutch Albertstalers which had become incredibly popular as trade coinage 
in the Baltic in the late 18th century. Also known as ‘ Albertsdalere’ , these Danish versions come with four different dates.
€3,500-€4,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Crown Prince Frederik’s travel to Norway in June 1788.
The beautiful type was created by the highly competent royal medalist, Adzer, who with understand

DENMARK. 20 Ducats, 1788-DIA/MF . Altona / Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. 
Off-strike in gold of Rigsdaler Courant struck for the personal use of the king and the crown prince. Fr-unlisted; KM-
unlisted (cf. 645 in silver); Hede-25; Sieg-19; Schou-7a; FP-E5 and p. 338; Bruun-7894. Weight: 69.22 gms. Mintmaster:

niably one of the most desirable of all in the L. E. Bruun collection. A magnific example of a truly imposing piece that

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 12 Skilling, 1812-IC. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 12 Skilling, 1812-IC. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
ik VI. NGC MS-64 Red. KM-673; Hede-11; Sieg-6.1; Schou-3; 
Bruun-8014. Mintmaster: Johannes Conradsen (IC). Struck on a 
Skilling 1771, KM-616 (note the inflation from 1 to 12 Skilling). 
The condition of this coin is breathtaking; a real showstopper.
The 1771 Skillings were recalled in 1812 and many were restruck 
as 12 Skillings. On this piece it is especially visible on the obverse.  
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Skilling, ND (1440-42). Lund Mint. Christopher III of Bavaria. NGC EF-45. Galster-18; Sieg-4; FL-11;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Skilling, ND (1440-42). Lund Mint. Christopher III of Bavaria. NGC EF-45. Galster-18; Sieg-4; FL-11; 
Jesse-705; Bruun-3763. Weight: 1.87 gms. Unusually attractive example of the first Danish Skilling. This denomination 
wasn’t discontinued until 1874.
The reverse legend: “GLORIA IN EXCELSIS DEO” was taken from the Song of Angels (Luke 2:14) 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
50. Dav-8229; Hede-17; Sieg-22; Schou-10; Aagaard & Märch -
er-72.1; Bruun-4525. Weight: 29.07 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Delhusen. Engraver: Cort Dellhusen. Very nice example struck 
on an unusually fine flan and boasting a charming, iridescent 
toning.
This beautiful type, which for the first time carries the three crowns 
of Sweden in the Danish royal arms, was struck from silver paid by 
the Swedish king to reacquire the fortress, Elfsborg, which had been 
conquered by the Danes during the Northern Seven Years War.  
€3,000-€4,000
From the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Tooled. Hede-23; Sieg-18;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Tooled. Hede-23; Sieg-18; 
Schou-2; Bruun-4534. Weight: 8.62 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. The pres-
ent example of this highly interesting type, which is presumed unique in private hands, was in the Royal Danish 
Collection in the mid 19th cent. before it was traded with Count Bille-Brahe. 
This type is from the first series struck at the newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of 
Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Denmark. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Half Portugaloser (5 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Re -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Half Portugaloser (5 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Re -
moved. Fr-63; Hede-6; Sieg-160; Harck-2.7; Schou-1; Bruun-4600. Weight: 17.32 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. 
Exceptionally well-struck on an even flan. With an outstanding issue as the present it is by no means surprising that 
there is evidence of a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as 
a medallion by high ranking dignitaries. In fact, most of the surviving Haderslev gold issues have traces of mounting. 
According to the most recent

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Hede-69; Sieg-63; Harck-4.2; Schou-5; Bru-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Hede-69; Sieg-63; Harck-4.2; Schou-5; Bru-
un-4625. Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. Very few examples are known in private hands of which 
this is far and away the best.
This historically important one-year type was struck as largesse money for the coronation on 29 August 1596.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2; 
Schou-8; Bruun-4650. Weight: 3.20 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A truly magnifi-
cent example with normally lost details and a blazing luster beneath an alluring iridescent toning. Scarce variant without 
curlicue below the elephant.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4668. Weight: 13.05 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A very nice example with all four corners 
sharply struck. Of the originally 143 examples minted few still exist.
Two different obverse dies were used with markedly different portraits of which this, resembling the one used for minor 
coins like the Soesling, Hede-82, is by far the rarest. At the time the value of 6 Daler corresponded to 3.5 Ducats (Hungarian 
Guldens).
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV .
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . 
NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. KM-33.1; Hede-92A; Sieg-65.1; 
Aagaard-2; Harck-6.32.1; Schou-1; Bruun-4705. Mintmaster: 
Nicolaus Schwabe. Minted from roller dies. An excellent portrait 
and sublime details combine to make this an absolutely amazing 
example of the characteristic type with the “hovering” crown. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff (Copenhagen - 1882) Lot # 433.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Skilling, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:

Harck-6.28.1; Schou-17; Bruun-4716. Mintmaster: Nicolaus 
Schwabe. A most impressive example that with little doubt is the 
finest in private ownership. For this very rare one-year type only 
one pair of dies were used.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:

flaw on the reverse is noted but otherwise a remarkably appeal-
ing example with an excellent old cabinet toning and a superior

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-48; KM-51;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-48; KM-51; 
Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-2; Bruun-4865. Weight: 8.93 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Delicate purplish hues 
further enhances the incredible eye-appeal of this magnificent piece boasting a splendid luster and wonderful details. 
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used 
in international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, 
“Gouden rijder” .

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-58. Dav-3525; KM-105; Hede-61A;

Taler with a remarkable luster and superlative details. The beautiful type with the curved, so-called “German” provincial 
shields is very rare.

which he had struck from Norwegian silver. Even though there might have been silver from other sources in the alloy ther

denmark.body_excerpt:

Hede-60B; Sieg-104.2; Harck-23.2; Schou-17; Bruun-5371. Weight:28.79 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A few 
minor obverse surface marks aside, this is an absolutely gorgeous example with a choice striking and a remarkable lus-
ter. The present specimen of this beautiful Taler type is with all likelihood unique as no other examples neither publicly 
held or in private collections have been recorded. 
The issue should probably be considered a pattern that was realized as the crown on the rev

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Krone, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3519; KM-96 (using a
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Krone, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3519; KM-96 (using a 
wrong illustration); Hede-125; Sieg-85; Harck-23.8; Schou-34; Bruun-5379. Weight: 18.62 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus 
Schwabe. An insignificant scratch can be seen underneath the crown on the reverse. This one-year type “broad” Krone is

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold 2 Krone, 1628. Copenhagen Mint; Privy mark: flower. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Obverse
denmark.body_excerpt:

Scratched. Fr-54; KM-74.2; Hede-25D; Sieg-134.4; Schou-5; Harck-29.1; Bruun-5542. Weight: 5.98 gms. Mintmaster: 
Peter Grüner. A superficial scratch can be seen in the reverse field above the crown, but otherwise a wonderfully lus -
trous example and decidedly better than the only other one known in private hands (Count Lerche Collection, 1992, lot 
661). This date is the only one struck for Peter Grüner who went on to become the mintmaster of Christiania, Norway 
from 1643 until his death

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -
tian IV . NGC Unc Details—Damaged, Cleaned. Fr-54; KM-
74.2; Hede-25E; Sieg-134.5; Schou-3; Bruun-5829. Weight: 5.91 
gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Five very small drill-holes 
are not easily explicable but might just have been done to test the 
gold. Otherwise a most attractive example perfectly struck on an 
even flan. Christian IV died early in 1648 (February 28th), and 
the present very rare Double Gold Krone is the only one minted 
by Heinrich Köhler.

ber a series of unusual coins were minted on square flans to be used 
as largesse money. Silver coins with a total value of 1000 Rigsdalers 
were minted in denominations of 1/4, 1/6 and 1/12 Speciedaler to 
be thrown to the crowd in the streets of Copenhagen. It has been 
speculate

han Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (obverse), Johan Blum

Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these 
Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade in the cen-
ter of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous 
Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1691. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-156; KM-415.1; Hede-31A; Sieg-104.1;

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:

Christianstad (Kristianstad) Fortress during the Scanian War.

is ultra rare.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. KM-cf. 479.2; Dav-1289; NMD-
denmark.body_excerpt:

1B; Hede-38; Sieg-23.3; Schou-9; Bruun-7335. Weight: 26.79 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. This is the

cisely struck example of one of the great rarities among the coins of Frederik IV . This Double Ducat featuring an elegant 
reverse design with an alternating pattern of crowned arms and monograms was minted in both 1708 and 1709 but no 
other examples of any of the dates are known to be in private ownership today with only one example of each date in

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1;
denmark.body_excerpt:

FP-47.4; Bruun-7974. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. A remarkable ex-
ample with an excellent luster.
The so-called “Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat type produced with the 1802 being the very last date of issue.

The beautifully rendered full-rigged ship on the reverse is used to symbolize the fact that the coin itself had been struck with 
gold brought back from overseas.

Bearing a symbolic reverse, the ship design on this type designates that the gold used to coin this piece was acquired from 
overseas. The Latin leg

alone should say it all, but when holding the piece in your hand you can almost feel the wind in your hair. A maritime Ducat from the 
very first tier.
Bearing a symbolic reverse, the ship design on this type designates that the gold used to coin this piece was acquired from overseas. The Latin 
legend, “DUCE PRUDENTIA, CONSTANTIA COMITE”, which has root

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Skilling, 1755. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65 Brown. KM-577.1; Hede-40A; Sieg-1.1; Schou-4; Bru-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Skilling, 1755. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65 Brown. KM-577.1; Hede-40A; Sieg-1.1; Schou-4; Bru-
un-7656. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai Van Hauen (PNVH). Engraver: Georg Wilhelm Wahl. Flaming red luster hugs the design, with 
sharp details throughout.
1/2 Skilling 1751 and 1755 were issued at the request of the governors of the poor houses, as a denomination smaller than 1 Skilling was 
needed for the church collection plates. The coins were recalled and demonetized in 1775. 
€800-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.body_excerpt:

Bruun-7667. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai van Hauen (VH); Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). 
An absolutely stunning Gem of this beautiful one-year type which is presumed to be the only Danish Ducat struck from 
gold mined in Norway. Excessively rare in such a wonderful condition.
Of the original mintage of 1,001 coins, 700 were immediately re-issued as Ducat Courants. With a gold content of c. 20% 
less, these were both more practical for circulation and more profitable

which - as opposed to practically all other Ducats - was actually 
struck for circulation, making it almost impossible to find in a 
sublime Gem condition like the present.

is bare but is here shown with a light drapery around his

denmark.body_excerpt:

Rethwisch castle was located between Lübeck and Hamburg in Holstein. The Rethwisch Mint, a subsidiary to the Royal Mint in Copenha-
gen, operated for only three years, 1768-1770, when it was moved to Altona. The coins minted at Re

of bankruptcy in 1813. 
€600-€800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Rigsbankdaler, 1834. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
ik VI. NGC MS-63 Prooflike. KM-706.2; Hede-27B; Sieg-28.2; 
Schou-6; Bruun-8093. Weight: 14.35 gms. Engraver: Hans 
Frederik Alsing (FA). Uncommon date and very difficult to find 
in the highest grades. Some light contact marks on the proof-like 
surfaces and a blue tinted toning spreading from the devices.

initials were replaced with ‘KM’ (Københavns Mønt / Copenhagen 
Mint) in 1833 and 1834.

bremen_verden.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1641. Bremervörde Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. KM-39; Dav-5077; Hede-
bremen_verden.body_excerpt:

In 1634 Frederik became archbishop of Bremen and the following year bishop of Verden. After the Torstenson 
Feud (part of the European Thirty Years’ War) and the Treaty of Brömsebro in 1645 both territories were however 
lost to Sweden.

bremen_verden.body_excerpt:

321; KM-61; SB-7a; Delzanno-78; Bruun Auction-1223; Bruun-12130. Weight: 21.77 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Möller. 
Excellent old toning on this scarce and historically interesting one-year type.
The Bishopric of Bremen & Verden were ceded to Sweden at the Westphalian Peace in 1648 and turned into a hereditary 
dukedom. Since the Hanseatic city of Bremen itself was separate from the bishopric, the Bishopric Mint was placed in the 
town of Stade. In 1712 the now dukedom was conquered by the Danes an

bremen_verden.meta_line: SWEDEN. Bremen & Verden. 4 Mark, 1670/69-AH. Stade Mint. NGC AU-53. KM-79; SB-16; Delzanno-631; Bru-
bremen_verden.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Bremen & Verden. 4 Mark, 1670/69-AH. Stade Mint. NGC AU-53. KM-79; SB-16; Delzanno-631; Bru-
un-12270. Weight: 22.13 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Hille. A rare coin with only very light wear; somewhat rusty dies but 
fully worthy of its grade.
The Duchy of Bremen & Verden was occupied by the Swedish forces in 1632 and officially ceded to Sweden on the Westpha-
lian Peace of 1648. It remained Swedish till 1720, when it was again ceded to the Elector of Hannover, Georg (George I of 
Great Britain) at the end of the Great Nordic War.
The mint was placed in Stade as the City of Bremen was not

defies words, and is a fitting tribute to Jacobsen, who only engraved Talers

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1794. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-67.  Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29;
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

Schou-1; FP-47.4; Bruun-7913. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Bauert. The 
so-called “Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat produced and the only one which gives the exact 
weight and fineness in the design, suiting it for international trade. Despite the ducat’s intended function 
of facilitating international trade, the present specimen clearly avoided any such usage and is as beautiful 
and charming as the day it was made. The Superb-Gem luster glows with a cutting

Gem, this Christian d’Or displays bright luster and sharply delineated details throughout. A piece largely 
without discernible fault, and as such, a golden example that stands near the pinnacle of desirability and

This coin marks the beginning of the Christian and Frederik d’ or co

Mintmaster: Didrik Christian Lyng. Engraver: Johan Henrik 
Wolff. This the last date of the Courant Ducats, which had been 
minted throughout the 18th century. It must have been one of 
the very last to be minted, as the die is both cracked and has 
many imperfections, most noticeably on the portrait.  
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 1/2 Talers, ND (1607-16). Johann Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-unlisted, cf.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 1/2 Talers, ND (1607-16). Johann Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-unlisted, cf. 
LS444; KM-unlisted, cf. 3; Sieg-12.4; Lange-cf. 264D; Bruun-14147. Weight: 73.07 gms. Excessively rare, unlisted as 2.5 
Taler in KM, Lange and Davenport. This spectacular and impressive 2.5 Taler is only listed in Sieg, who were aware of 
the L. E. Bruun Collection. The closest we can come to anything like this coin is a 1.5 Taler from the same dies, which 
was sold in 2021. The present coin has wonderful, original surfaces and plenty of luster.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L.E. Bruun Col

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Taler, 1674-AH. Stade Mint. Christian Albrecht. NGC Unc Details—
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

Cleaned. Dav-3704; KM-120; Sieg-7.1; Lange-375; Bruun-14419. Weight: 28.88 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Hille. Ex-
tremely rare single-year Taler issue. Lustrous with some smoothing in obverse fields; the reverse uncirculated with 
beautiful, subtle toning; exquisite detailing throughout. Only one other example is listed as sold (twice) on CoinAr -
chives, this present example being in far better condition.
The shield on the reverse holds the arms of Norway, Schleswig, Holstein, Stormarn, and Dit

better than any other, this cataloguer has been able to find.

From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Mark, 1681.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Mark, 1681. 
Schleswig Mint. Christian Albrecht. NGC EF Details—
Cleaned. KM-133; Sieg-15; Lange-404; Bruun-14435. Weight: 
10.93 gms. Scarce one-year type and one of only two Marks to 
ever come out of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lightly double-
struck and with a touch of softness at the center, otherwise clear 
and legible and of a highly unusual design. Old toning accentu-
ates the devices.
The mintmaster is unknown and hides behind the enigmatic, in-
cuse hourglass mintmark. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, ND (1681-
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

unlisted (cf. 577); KM-unlisted (cf. 136); Sieg-16.1; Lange-399C; 
Bruun-14473. Weight: 22.00 gms. Mintmaster: Claus Jakob 
Mecklenburg. Excessive rare ND issue with the Duke’s Motto 
‘Per Aspera Ad Astra’ on the reverse instead of denomination 
and date. Excellent surfaces with old toning.
The famous Christian Lange Collection only had a rubbing of the 
example in the Danish National Museum and knew of one more 
example, sold in Copenhagen in 1871 as Lot #2612 at C. J. Thom-
sen’s

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, 1698. Tönning Mint. Friedrich IV . NGC EF-45. Dav-579; KM-
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

166; Sieg-7; Lange-430AA; Bruun-14494. Weight: 22.00 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Heinrich Luders. Excessively rare sin-
gle-year emission; Mottled toning with a weakness to the edge that interferes with the legends on both sides.
The scarcity of this coin is illustrated not only by a lack of recorded sales on CoinArchives but also by the fact that Lange 
only had a rubbing of the example in the Danish National Museum.

From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Mark, 1698. Tön -

430A; Bruun-14495. Weight: 10.84 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Heinrich Luders. Very rare single-year emission. Excellent de-
tails with a slight weakness as often seen on the coins from that 
mint, and an old, uneven toning.
Mark emissions from Schleswig-Holstein are rare, this being the 
second of only two. 
€2,000-€2,400

Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler,
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler, 
1627//1626. Reinfeldt or Sonderburg Mint. Alexander. NGC 
VF Details—Cleaned. Dav-3717; KM-28; Sieg-5; Lange-560; 
Bruun-14678. Weight: 27.26 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Lilien-
thal. Very rare Taler struck on the death of Alexander in 1627. 
Unevenly toned but gleaming with multicolor. An interesting 
zwitter/mule in that the reverse die of the 1626 Taler was reused 
on this coin, giving it two different dates.
The Norwegian lion sits atop the reverse arms as the dukes bore the 
hereditary title of ‘heir to Norway’ or ‘Heres Norvegie’. 
€3,000-€4,0

With only one reco

schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

28.46 gms. Mintmaster: Thomas Eisenbein. Rare Death Taler; 
very weak in the most central parts and some double striking 
in the obverse legend but the coin seems far better than the VF 
grade, and a dark, old toning with hints of blue accentuates the 
devices for much eye-appeal. 
€1,800-€2,200
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg. 
1/2 Taler, ND (1601-22). Altona Mint. Ernst III. NGC AU-53. 
KM-102; Lange cf. 920; Weinmeister-159; Bruun-14918. Weight: 
14.27 gms. Extremely rare Half Taler in extraordinary condition. 
An old, dark patina covers the surfaces and gives the coin, along 
with the colorful toning, a most sublime expression. Light wear 
to the high points but well-struck and with excellent details.
Portrait coins from Ernst III are all very rare; the sublime portrait 
and spectacular condition of this coin makes it a true unicorn. 
€1,000-€1,400
From the L.E.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1646/5. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Dav-3670; KM-27; Hede-163C; Sieg-186.3;
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1646/5. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Dav-3670; KM-27; Hede-163C; Sieg-186.3; 
Harck-38.4.3; Schou-35; Lange-40b; Bruun-5801. Weight: 28.75 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger (IS). Engraver: signed (IL). A most 
pleasing piece of this ever popular type that is rarely encountered in MS condition.

tian IV as a metaphor when the city of Glückstadt was founded in 1616. 
€2,500-€3,500

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Rigsbankskilling, 1842. Altona Mint. Christian VIII. NGC MS-66. KM-721.3; Hede-8C; Sieg-8.3; Schou-6; Bru -
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Rigsbankskilling, 1842. Altona Mint. Christian VIII. NGC MS-66. KM-721.3; Hede-8C; Sieg-8.3; Schou-6; Bru -
un-8152. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund (FF). Engraver: Frederik Christopher Krohn (FK). NGC TOP POP . Extremely rare 
variation struck in Altona in the Duchies. 
While more than one million were minted of this type in 1842, only a handful are known to have survived. The majority were not put into 
circulation but kept as a reserve in Altona. When the city fell to the rebels in 1848, the coins were seized and melted down. 
€3,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Weight: 13.27 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund (FF). Engraver: Frederik Christopher Krohn (FK). NGC TOP POP . Rare, one-
year type with silky surfaces, a few small bagmarks.
The Christian and Frederik d’Or coinage The first minting of d’Or coinage in Denmark took place in 1771 and 1775 but it is believed to be 
a coinage for a single specific use the Duchies. 1826 saw a few Double d’Ors minted, but from 1827 minting took place on a larger scale. The 
coins, imitating

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich.
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich. 
NGC MS-62. Dav-5442; KM-63; Lange-504c; Bruun-15028. Weight: 29.56 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Ridder. Extremely 
rare Taler in excellent condition and of the highest historical importance to match its Mint State grade. The number 
‘3325’ written ink on the obverse refers to its number in ‘Madai’s Thaler-Cabinett’ from 1765.
Listed in Bruun’s catalogue as 1688 but appears to be 1683 over 1678.
€3,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Ludwig de Traux Collection (Vienna - 1857), Lot3 4

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Ducat, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Ducat, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC 
MS-62. Fr-1508; KM- 80.2; Lange-510AAb; Bruun-15044. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. Excessively rare Ducat, only 50 struck, and missing from the auction circuit for decades if not 
longer. Even Lange only had a galvanic copy in his collection. A magnificent looking piece with full luster and lots of 
eye-appeal, this coin is the perfect combination of beauty, rarity and conservation.
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC 
MS-62. Dav-2409; KM-79; Lange-511; Bruun-15046. Weight: 29.22 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. A great rarity in incredible condition.
The Younger brother of Friedrich IV of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp, Christian Albert was given the small duchy of Eutin 
and was elected Bishop of Lübeck in 1706. He served as co-ruler in Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp from the death of his brother 
until the coming-of-age of his nephew, Karl Friedrich, in 1717.
€8,000-€10,0

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
rich August. NGC MS-61. Fr-1509 and 1931; KM-87 (Lübeck); Trippler/Kalvelage-369; Lange-522; Bruun-15142. 
Weight: 6.66 gms. Mintmaster: Casper Heinrich Lyng. Engraver: Johann Ephraim Bauert. Very rare, only 580 struck; 
200 in 1776 and a further 380 in 1781 using the same dies, making any distinction impossible. Superb condition, the 
second highest ranking on the NGC census.
Struck by Friedrich August as both Duke of Oldenburg and Bishop of Lübeck. His coinage can therefore be fou

Lange-cf. 492C; Bruun-14975. Weight: 28.49 gms. Extremely 
rare date and type. Lovely, dark toning with hints of gold and 
multicolor and only slight wear to the high points. Some marks 
in the obverse fields.

GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC AU-53. Dav-623; KM-78.1; 
Lange-511A; Bruun-15039. Weight: 17.43 gms. Mintmaster: An-
dreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden as seen by the 
lack of a photo or even a drawing of it in Davenport. The surfaces 
are uncleaned with a greenish tint hiding promises of luster, and 
while there is a softness to the top points in the portrait it seems 
to be a weakness in the strike rather than wear or possibly a com-
bination.
Christian August of Holstein-Gottorp-Eutin was the grandson on 
his mother’s sid

GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC MS-62. Dav-623; KM-78.2; 
Lange-cf. 511A; Bruun-15040. Weight: 17.10 gms. Mintmaster: 
Andreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden in extraordi-
nary condition; old, greenish tinted toning covers the uncircu -
lated surfaces with luster shimmering underneath. A die crack 
runs through the portrait. The elegant details in the hair shows 
off not only the artistry of the die cutter but also the condition of 
the piece, as there are no flattened part, neither from weak strike 
nor wear.
No recorded sales

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411;
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411; 
KM-85; Trippler/Kalvelage-371; Lange 523; Bruun-15140. Weight: 28.89 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Heinrich Lyng. Excel-
lent condition for type with playful luster around the devices and attractive, old toning; only 1,000 pieces struck.
Struck to Danish standard in 1777 re-using the reverse of the Albertstaler of 1775, the obverse bears the title ‘DVX REGN 
OLD’ - ‘Duke Regent of Oldenburg’, a title he was only granted in 1776.
Friedrich August only had three coins struck: the 1776 gold Pistol
